coredata.dc:description: Reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH) and its phosphate ester (NADPH) participate in numerous metabolic processes in living cells as electron carriers. The levels of NADH and NADPH in a cell are closely related to its metabolic and pathological state. It is important to monitor the levels of NADH and NADPH in living cells and in vivo in real-time. This review mainly focuses on fluorescent probes developed for monitoring NADH and NADPH in living cells and in vivo, and classifies them according to the recognition units. These fluorescence probes can rapidly respond to changes in NADH and NADPH levels without interference from other biomolecules, both in cell culture and in vivo. These probes have been employed to monitor NADH and NADPH levels in living cells, tumor spheroids, and in vivo; moreover, some of them can be used to discriminate normal cells from cancer cells, and detect cancer cell death due to reductive stress induced by natural antioxidants. This review is expected to inspire the generation of novel fluorescent probes for the detection of NADH and NADPH, and stimulate more attention in the development of fluorescent probes based on carbon dots and nanoparticles, as well as metal complex-based, time-gated luminescent probes for monitoring NADH and NADPH in both living cells and in vivo.

head.ce:title: The recent development of fluorescent probes for the detection of NADH and NADPH in living cells and in vivo

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH) and its phosphate ester (NADPH) participate in numerous metabolic processes in living cells as electron carriers. The levels of NADH and NADPH in a cell are closely related to its metabolic and pathological state. It is important to monitor the levels of NADH and NADPH in living cells and in vivo in real-time. This review mainly focuses on fluorescent probes developed for monitoring NADH and NADPH in living cells and in vivo, and classifies them according to the recognition units. These fluorescence probes can rapidly respond to changes in NADH and NADPH levels without interference from other biomolecules, both in cell culture and in vivo. These probes have been employed to monitor NADH and NADPH levels in living cells, tumor spheroids, and in vivo; moreover, some of them can be used to discriminate normal cells from cancer cells, and detect cancer cell death due to reductive stress induced by natural antioxidants. This review is expected to inspire the generation of novel fluorescent probes for the detection of NADH and NADPH, and stimulate more attention in the development of fluorescent probes based on carbon dots and nanoparticles, as well as metal complex-based, time-gated luminescent probes for monitoring NADH and NADPH in both living cells and in vivo.

ce:section.ce:para: Reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (NADH) and its phosphate ester (NADPH) are important coenzymes in living cells and participate in numerous cellular metabolic processes, including energy metabolism, mitochondrial functions, immune function, biosynthesis, gene expression, cell death, and aging [1–5]. NADH/NAD+ and NADPH/NADP+ are key electron carriers in living cells, coupling the transfer of hydrogen atoms and electrons from one metabolite to another in many cellular redox reactions [6]. NADH and NADPH are involved in the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) and reactive nitrogen species (RNS), and also play important roles in the metabolism of reactive sulfur species (RSS) as well as other important biological species [7–9]. Changes in NADH and NADPH levels are closely related to many metabolic pathological states, such as diabetes and cancer. The spatiotemporal tracking of NADPH metabolism in living cells is crucial for understanding the role of this cofactor in cell biology [10]. For example, in cancer cells, an oncogene-driven abnormal increase in glycolysis leads to the overproduction of NADH. This highlights that real-time, dynamic monitoring of NADH and NADPH in living cells is critical for investigating and understanding the contributions of NADH and NADPH to both physiological and pathological processes.

To date, many methods have been developed to detect NADH and NADPH in vitro, including the enzymatic cycling assay [10], electrochemical analysis [11,12], high-performance liquid chromatography [13], and capillary electrophoresis [14]. However, these methods have limitations in detecting changes in NADH and NADPH levels in living cells and tissues in real-time [15]. Fluorescent probes have gained broad interest from the scientific community owing to their many advantages, such as their high sensitivity [16,17], high selectivity [18–20], rapid response rate, low detection limit [21–23], and ease of manipulation [24]. In the past few decades, attention has focused on the development of fluorescent probes for the detection of NADH and NADPH in living cells, and even in vivo [25–27]. These probes have been successfully employed for distinguishing normal cells from cancer cells, and detecting cancer cell death through reductive stress [28,29] induced by natural antioxidants. This review summarizes recently developed NADH and NADPH probes according to the different recognition units.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent probes for the detection of NADH and NADPH in cells and in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescent probes usually consist of two main parts, i.e., a signal reporter unit and a recognition unit [30]. Recognition units can selectively bind to, or react with the target analyte, resulting in changes in the fluorescence intensity or absorption/emission wavelengths of the fluorophore [31–33]. Selective and sensitive detection of analytes can be achieved with high temporal and spatial resolution [34]. A key feature of NADH and NADPH is their high reducibility, and both coenzymes can reduce many electron-deficient heterocyclic compounds such as quinone, resazurin, quinolinium and pyridine, which thus often serve as different recognition units in fluorescent probes. So far, several fluorescent probes have been successfully developed for the detection of NADH and NADPH [35–38] by combining the strong reducibility of NADH and NADPH with classical fluorescence sensing mechanisms such as photo-induced electron transfer (PET), intramolecular charge transfer (ICT), and the spirolactone ring open–close mechanism (Scheme 1 ). Herein we will summarize the recent progress of this field.

Quinone, a typical recognition unit for NADH and NADPH, can be linked to xanthene fluorophores such as fluorescein, rhodamine and rhodol, forming the corresponding fluorescent probes. Although fluorescein was first synthesized in 1871, it remains one of the most widely employed fluorophores. The fluorescence quantum yield of fluorescein is as high as 0.95 in alkaline solutions. In contrast, fluorescein is almost nonfluorescent in acid solutions due to the protonation of its phenolic hydroxyl group. This unique feature makes fluorescein an ideal platform for the design of turn-on fluorescent probes [39]. However, fluorescein exhibits low cell permeability, and, to overcome this disadvantage, many fluorescein-based probes are further modified to their ester forms. Rhodamines are the amino isologues of fluorescein, and possess excellent photophysical properties, such as high fluorescence quantum yield [40], high photostability, and a large absorption coefficient [41]. Importantly, rhodamine compounds can easily penetrate cells, rendering rhodamine-based probes highly suitable for practical applications in living cells [42]. Rhodol is the hybrid form of fluorescein and rhodamine [43]. Like fluorescein and rhodamine, it can also exist in a ring-open or ring-closed form [44]. Consequently, rhodol dyes are widely employed in the design of fluorescent probes.

Using quinone as a recognition unit, Komatsu et al. [45] reported a xanthene-based fluorescent probe (1) for detecting NADH and NADPH in living cells (Fig. 1 ). The sensitivity of probe 1 could be enhanced in the presence of an iridium (Ir) complex as a promoter. Probe 1 emitted a strong fluorescence signal at 518 nm. However, the fluorescence intensity was gradually weakened due to the reduction of quinone to phenolic hydroxyl in the presence of NADPH. The fluorescence response toward NADPH was measured in the presence or absence of a promoter or the presence of quinone reductase in PBS. The fluorescence intensity in the presence of the promoter decreased after the addition of 1 mM NADPH. These results indicated that the promoter could activate NADPH and enhance the reaction rate between probe 1 and NADPH. The fluorescence intensity of probe 1 showed a gradual and linear decrease with increasing NADPH concentrations in the range of 0.5–5.0 mM. Surprisingly, the fluorescence intensity was correlated with the concentrations of NADPH in this range. The fluorescence response of probe 1 toward NADH was similar to that toward NADPH under the same condition, except that the reaction rate was slightly slower. This may be due to the negatively charged phosphate group in NADPH binding to the positively charged Ir complex through electrostatic attraction. Probe 1 had excellent selectivity for NADH and NADPH. Confocal microscopy analysis showed that most of probe 1 localized in the cytosol. When cells that had been incubated with probe 1 were treated with the promoter, a rapid increase in fluorescence was initially observed, followed by a gradual decrease to a very low level. The rapid increase in fluorescence may result from injection shock. The authors also showed that the appearance of the fluorescence in the cell after 4 min was due to the presence of the promoter, and that cell death occurred after 7 min. Cell death may be ascribed to the NADH- and NADPH-mediated reduction of ubiquinone or reduced levels of NADH and NADPH, which would have affected cell homeostasis and led to cell necrosis.

Resorufin is another typical azaxanthene fluorescent dye with a wide absorption spectrum and emission wavelengths above 550 nm [46,47]. Like fluorescein, the hydroxyl group of resorufin undergoes protonation and deprotonation in aqueous solution with on–off fluorescence switching. Moreover, the protection and deprotection of the 7-hydroxy substitution of resorufin also result in a change in fluorescence [48]. By taking the advantage of this property, various fluorescent probes have been developed [49,50]. Importantly, resazurin, the oxidative derivative of resorufin, can be reduced by NADH and NADPH in the presence of enzymes, and has been applied as a fluorescent probe for the detection of reductases [51,52].

Wang et al. [53] developed a turn-on fluorescent probe (2) for the detection of NADH based on a combination-reaction, two-step sensing strategy. The resazurin moiety served both as recognition site and fluorophore (Fig. 2 ). In the first step, the boronic acid of probe 2 combined with the ribose of NADH to shorten the spatial distance and accelerate the sensing process. In the second step, the active hydrogen from NADH was transferred to the probe, thereby promoting the reduction of weakly fluorescence probe 2 and the generation of strongly fluorescent products. The fluorescence intensity gradually increased at the maximum wavelength with a blue-shift from 620 to 575 nm in a buffer solution (pH = 9.5). The fluorescence intensity of probe 2 showed an excellent linear correlation with NADH concentrations in the 0–1 μM range, and the detection limit was 0.087 μM. The fluorescence signal of probe 2 toward NADH was stronger under conditions of high pH values (pH 9.5–11.5), likely because an alkaline environment was more conducive to the binding of the probe to NADH. Probe 2 only responded to NADH in various analytes at 575 nm. Notably, probe 2 had a relatively weak fluorescence response to NADPH, possibly because the phosphorylation group of NADPH hindered the binding of ribose to boric acid in probe 2. However, although probe 2 could detect NADH in buffer solution, the weak response of this probe in an alkaline environment still limited its application in vivo. Therefore, to adapt to a physiological environment, the authors transformed the structure of boronic acid in probe 2 into boronic esters, forming probe 3. Probe 3 also had a fluorescence response to NADH at pH 7.4, with a detection limit as low as 0.41 μM, and presented a similar experimental response to that of 2. Probe 3 exhibited low toxicity against cells as measured by an MTT assay. When oral squamous cell carcinoma (OSCC) cells were first pretreated with different concentrations of glucose for 30 min, and then incubated with probe 3 (5 μM) for 15 min, the fluorescence of glucose pretreated cells was stronger than that of the control, untreated cells because glucose can produce NADH through glycolysis [54]. Moreover, this effect was glucose concentration-dependent. Subsequently, cells were preincubated with 10 mM lactate and a mixture of 10 mM lactate and 5 mM pyruvate for 10 min. The results revealed that the lactate-treated cells presented substantially stronger fluorescence than those treated with lactate and pyruvate, indicating that the combination of lactate and pyruvate influenced the intracellular NADH levels. Similar phenomena were also observed in Chinese hamster ovary cell lines (CHO) and human cervical cancer cells (HCE 1). This study provided a new, practical method for improving the sensitivity and selectivity of fluorescent probes for sensing complex biomolecules and an excellent tool for the study of complicated biomolecules.

The well-known Nile red and Nile blue dyes belong to the benzo[a]phenoxazine family of fluorophores [55]. These fluorophores feature a long emission wavelength, large Stokes shift, and polarity-sensitive fluorescence [56]. Notably, Nile red is the most utilized commercial probe for the detection of lipid droplets. Various fluorescent probes have been designed through the modification of the hydroxyl or amino group of benzo[a]phenoxazine [57,58]. Santra et al. [59] reported the synthesis of a benzophenoxazine-based, red-emitting fluorescent probe (4) for the reversible detection of NADH, NADPH, and HOCl (Fig. 3 ). Probe 4 contained an N-(3H-phenoxazin-3-ylidene)aminium moiety that could react with NADH and NADPH to form the corresponding 10H-phenoxazin-3-amine, which was accompanied by fluorescence turn-off at 650 nm. Obviously, benzophenoxazine served as not only the recognition site but also the fluorophore. However, fluorescence could be quantitatively restored through HOCl-mediated oxidation. The fluorescence intensity of probe 4 remained unchanged in the presence of other related biomolecules, except for NADH and NADPH, indicating that this probe recognized NADH and NADPH. Glucose-incubated cells can produce NADH (i.e., increasing NADH levels) due to glycolysis. When HeLa cells were preincubated with probe 4 (10 μM) and then treated with different concentrations of glucose (0, 10, 20, and 40 mM), the fluorescence signal was weaker in cells incubated with 40 mM glucose than in the cells from the other groups, indicating that the change in the fluorescence intensity was indeed due to the presence of NADH. This was the first time that a probe had been used for cycle fluorescence detection of NADH, NADPH, and HOCl.

Cyanine dyes consist of a polymethine chain and two nitrogen-containing heterocycles, one of which is positively charged [60]. Cyanine dyes have attracted increasing attention due to their relatively wide absorption spectra and long emission wavelengths [60]. The introduction of a vinylene moiety or nitrogen-containing heterocycles into the backbone of these chromophores can induce an absorption and emission redshift [61]. Although cyanine is one of the most widely used fluorophores, the low quantum yield in aqueous solution limits its practical applications to some extent [62–64].

Fomin et al. [65] reported a probe (5) with two acceptors for detecting NADH and NADPH under physiological conditions (Fig. 4 ). The quinolinium moiety served as the recognition site for NADH and NADPH, as well as a component of the cyanine fluorophore. The sensing mechanism involved the active hydrogen transferred from NADH and NADPH attacking the 4-position of the quinolinium moiety, which resulted in a new cyanine donor–π–acceptor structure and the emission of a fluorescence signal. The main absorption peaks of probe 5 were at 312 and 384 nm, which red-shifted to 537 nm after the addition of NADH and NADPH. The fluorescence intensity increased within 5 min at 561 nm in the presence of NADH and NADPH. The absorbance intensity showed a good linear relationship with NADH concentrations in the range of 0.3–20.0 μM, with a detection limit of 0.1 μM. Probe 5 showed excellent selectivity toward NADH when compared with other redox-active analytes, including reduced nicotinamide adenine dinucleotide (FADH2), cysteine (Cys), and ascorbic acid (AA). Confocal microscopy analysis showed that probe 5 was mostly distributed in the cytoplasm, and to a lesser extent in mitochondria. The reduction products of probe 5 displayed a photostability similar to that of reference dye TMRM in HCT116 cells. Subsequently, wild-type and SCO2−/− knockout (inactivated oxidative phosphorylation) HCT116 cells were stained with probe 5. Cell imaging showed that SCO2−/− cells emitted a stronger fluorescence signal than the wild-type cells, likely because the latter used both oxidative phosphorylation and glycolysis, whereas oxidative phosphorylation was inactive in SCO2−/− cells, resulting in higher NADH and NADPH levels. Furthermore, wild-type or SCO2−/− cell-derived tumor spheroids were incubated with probe 5. The NADH and NADPH fluorescence gradient at the periphery and core was greater in wild-type cell spheroids than in SCO2−/− cell spheroids, likely because of the lower metabolic activity in the hypoxic core of the SCO2−/− cell spheroids which would lead to the reduced production of NADH and NADPH. Probe 5 can be used for the detection of NADH and NADPH in cells and 3D tumor spheroids.

Podder et al. [66] reported a turn-on fluorescent probe (6) by employing quinolinium as a recognition site and rhodamine as a fluorophore (Fig. 5 ). Alone, probe 6 emitted a weak fluorescence signal at 580 nm in PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4). The active hydrogen atom from NADH attacked the aromatic π-bond at C4 of the quinoline moiety of probe 6 to form a reduced product, which “turned on” the fluorescence. Furthermore, the fluorescence intensity increased with time (0–4 h). A linear relationship was observed between the emission intensity and NADH concentrations in the range of 0–400 μM. The fluorescence signal of probe 6 was free from interference by other biological reductants, such as glutathione (GSH), AA, Cys, FADH2, and Fe2+. The above results showed that probe 6 had good sensitivity and excellent selectivity toward NADH. Probe 6 exhibited low toxicity against A549 cells at high concentrations (up to 50 μM) as measured by the WST-1 assay. Colocalization experiments with probe 6 and Mito-Tracker, ER-Tracker, or Lyso-Tracker showed that probe 6 did not colocalize with any of the organelle trackers, but did colocalize with cytosolic NADH. The fluorescence signal in A549 cells gradually increased with increasing doses of NADH (0–5 mM), indicating that probe 6 can be applied to monitor NADH in live cells. Subsequently, probe 6 fluorescence staining was examined in A549 cells under different oxygen concentrations (1–21%). The fluorescence intensity gradually increased as the oxygen concentration decreased. Moreover, A549 cells were pretreated with 0–20 mM glucose and 0–5 mM pyruvate, and then treated with probe 6. The fluorescence signal in A549 cells gradually increased with increasing glucose concentrations, but decreased with increasing pyruvate levels. This was because glucose could generate NADH during glycolysis. Pyruvate consumed NADH and was converted to lactic acid under the action of lactate dehydrogenase. To verify this possibility, A549 cells were preincubated with 0–20 μM NHI-2 (a lactate dehydrogenase-A inhibitor) for 2 h. The results showed increased fluorescence intensity with increasing concentrations of NHI-2, indicating that pyruvate could consume NADH in cells under the action of propionate dehydrogenase. These findings demonstrated that probe 6 could be applied to monitor NADH in live cells following various metabolic alterations.

Hemicyanines are a typical donor–π–acceptor (D–π–A) type of fluorophores. These fluorophores consist of a positively charged nitrogen heterocyclic moiety and an amino or hydroxy group as the electron acceptor and electron donor, respectively [60]. The intramolecular charge transfer of hemicyanine can be modulated by modifying the electron-donating and electron-withdrawing groups, leading to the emission of different fluorescence signals that can be used for sensing purposes. Many new types of hemicyanines have been developed and widely utilized in the design of fluorescent probes, including coumarin hemicyanine [67], Changsha (CS), and Huda (HD)–near-infrared (NIR) dyes [68–70].

To the best of our knowledge, the study by Zhao et al. [71] was the first report of real-time fluorescent imaging of endogenous NADH and NADPH in tumor-bearing mice. Probe 7 contains dicyanoisophorone as an NIR chromophore and 1-methylquinolinium cation as a recognition unit of NADH and NADPH (Fig. 6 ). Probe 7 displays a turn-on NIR fluorescence response to NADH and NADPH. The active hydrogen atom from NADH or NADPH attacks the aromatic π-bond at C4 of the quinoline moiety to form a reduction product possessing an intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) process, thereby emitting a distinct NIR fluorescence signal. Whereas probe 7 exhibited a negligible fluorescent signal, the fluorescence intensity of probe 7 (10 μM) was significantly enhanced at 660 nm after treatment with NADH (50 μM). Probe 7 also showed a time-dependent (0–30 min) fluorescence response to NADH (50 μM). The fluorescence intensity at 660 nm was gradually enhanced with increasing concentrations of NADH (0–1 μM) and displayed a good linear relationship with NADH concentrations ranging from 0 to 1 μM, exhibiting a detection limit as low as 12 nM. The fluorescence intensity of probe 7 increased markedly only after the addition of NADH and NADPH in the presence of related species, including inorganic salts, common amino acids, and reducing agents. An MTT assay was used to confirm that HepG-2 cells retained more than 95% viability for 12 h, even at a concentration of 50 μM probe. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) can consume NADH in cells via H2O2-induced oxidative stress [72]. When HepG-2 cells were pretreated with H2O2 (100 μM) and glucose (20 mM), and then incubated with probe 7 (10 μM), the fluorescence signal of cells pretreated with H2O2 was significantly reduced, whereas that of glucose-pretreated cells was increased. These results indicated that probe 7 could be successfully used for the detection of intracellular NADH and NADPH. Colocalization analysis suggested that probe 7 was specifically captured by mitochondria in living cells, indicating that probe 7 could also be applied to detect NADH and NADPH in these organelles. When HepG-2 cells and HL-7702 cells were incubated with probe 7 (10 μM) for 15 min, the fluorescence signal was stronger in HepG-2 cells than in HL-7702 cells, indicating that probe 7 could be used to distinguish normal cells from cancer cells. When tumor-bearing Kunming mice were orthotopically injected with buffer solutions containing probe 7 (10 μM), the fluorescence signal (using a small animal in vivo imaging system) in the tumor region gradually increased with time. Probe 7 could also rapidly detect NADH and NADPH inside tumor tissue due to its strong cell-penetrating ability.

Maiti et al. [74] developed a rapid and selective fluorescent probe (9) for detecting NADH by employing quinolinium as the recognition unit (Fig. 8 ). Probe 9 emitted a strong fluorescence signal at 552 nm that increased with increasing doses of NADH in PBS (pH 7.4), with a detection limit of 1.25 μM. The fluorescence intensity of probe 9 (5 μM) increased gradually after the addition of NADH in 0–60 min. The detection of NADH was not affected by other related biomolecules, such as FADH2, Cys, GSH, AA, trypsin, tyrosinase, and H2O2. Probe 9 exhibited low toxicity against cells as demonstrated by an MTT assay. Colocalization experiments showed that probe 9/NADH were mainly localized to the cytoplasm. When HeLa, MDA MB-231, and WI-38 cells were treated with different concentrations of d-glucose (0–20 mM) and pyruvate (0–10 mM), and then incubated with probe 9, the fluorescence intensity of the cells increased gradually with increasing D-glycolysis in the three types of cells. In contrast, the fluorescence signal decreased with increasing pyruvate levels in the three cell types. Furthermore, when HeLa cells were treated with lactic acid or a mixture of pyruvate and lactic acid, and then incubated with probe 9, the former showed stronger fluorescence intensity than the latter, indicating that probe 9 could be used as an indicator of the metabolic status in living cells via monitoring the levels of NADH and NADPH. Finally, HeLa cells were pretreated with GSH (0–10 mM) and buthionine sulphoximine (BSO; 0–5 mM), and then incubated with 10 μM probe 9. The fluorescence intensity of the cells decreased with increasing concentrations of GSH; however, the fluorescence signal of the cells gradually increased after the addition of BSO, which restricted GSH biosynthesis. Collectively, these observations indicated that probe 9 can be used to detect changes in intracellular NADH levels induced by GSH fluctuations in living cells.

Coumarins are found in numerous natural products, and have a characteristic 2H-chromen-2-one motif. Studies on their photophysical properties have shown that substitution with an electron-withdrawing group at the 3-position and/or an electron-donating group at the 7-position strongly enhances their fluorescence intensities [75]. Coumarins have many advantages, such as large Stokes shifts, excellent photostability, and low toxicity [76]. However, the absorption and emission wavelengths of coumarin fluorophores are located within the visible range, which restricts their application for in vivo imaging.

With quinolinium as a recognition unit, Joo et al. [77] designed a far-red fluorescent probe (10) for the detection of NADH and NADPH in live cells and 3D tumor spheroids (Fig. 9 ). Probe 10 had a dicyanocoumarin-fused quinolinium structure with a donor–acceptor–acceptor (D–A–A) system, and could form a D–A–D structure after reacting with NADH or NADPH, thereby emitting a strong fluorescence signal due to the ICT effect. The fluorescence intensity of probe 10 gradually increased within 0–80 min in the presence of 50 μM NADH. The fluorescence intensity was enhanced with increasing concentrations of NADH in the range of 0–70 μM, with a detection limit of 0.1 μM. Probe 10 presented a strong fluorescence signal at 640 nm only after the addition of NADH and NADPH, indicating it has excellent selectivity for the two coenzymes. Additionally, the fluorescent signal of probe 10 increased within a pH range of 5–8 in the presence of NADH (50 μM). These results indicated that probe 10 could function under different cellular pH conditions via off–on changes in the fluorescence signal. Furthermore, probe 10 exhibited low toxicity against MDA-MB-231 cells, as demonstrated by an MTT assay, and the cells maintained more than 95% viability even at a concentration of 20 μM. Next, the cells were pretreated with 20 mM glucose and 5 mM pyruvate, and then incubated with probe 10. The fluorescence signal of cells pretreated with glucose was stronger than that of cells in the control group. In contrast, pyruvate-pretreated cells showed a weaker fluorescent signal relative to the control group. The above results confirmed the ability of probe 10 to detect NADH and NADPH in living cells. Cisplatin was reported to inhibit cell growth and proliferation by inhibiting the expression of glycolysis-related proteins [78]. The NADH and NADPH levels would decrease as glycolysis is inhibited. Therefore, MDA-MB-231 cells were pretreated with cisplatin (20 μM) for 6 and 24 h and then incubated with probe 10 (5 μM) for 15 min. The fluorescence intensity showed a gradual decrease with increasing treatment time, and the fluorescent signal disappeared after 24 h when compared with the control, untreated group. These results demonstrated that probe 10 can be applied to study the metabolic changes occurring in cancer cells by monitoring the levels of NADH and NADPH. Finally, MDA-MB-231 cells were first pretreated with normoxia (21% 02) and hypoxia (1% 02) for 12 h and then incubated with probe 10 for 5 min. The fluorescence intensity of the cells was much stronger under hypoxic conditions than under normoxic conditions. Furthermore, a 3D tumor sphere model was established in MDA-MB-231 cells, and the spheres were incubated with 10 μM probe 10 for 30 min. Probe 10 displayed a strong fluorescence signal in the internal area (40–80 μm) of the tumor spheroids, whereas no fluorescence was observed in the external regions (0–20 μm and 100–120 μm) of the tumor spheroids, indicating that the observed fluorescence was present in an oxygen-deficient environment (between 40 and 80 μm deep). These results further illustrated that probe 10 could be used as an indicator of hypoxic status through the detection of NADH and NADPH fluorescence intensity in both living cells and tumor spheroids. Probe 10 could be successfully used to detect specific biological processes and under a hypoxic environment.

Joo et al. [79] reported a pyridinium-based, mitochondria-targetable red-emitting fluorescent probe (11) for the real-time detection of NADH and NADPH in living cells (Fig. 10 ). In probe 11, the cationic triphenylphosphine moiety served as the mitochondrial targeting group, and the pyridinium moiety as a recognition unit and a part of fluorophore that could be attacked by the active hydrogen of NADH and NADPH, which would result in the formation of a π-conjugated electron donor–acceptor structure and the generation of a strong fluorescence signal. Probe 11 had almost no fluorescence. However, it exhibited bright red fluorescence at 615 nm in the presence of NADH with increasing time (from 0 to 23 h) after the addition of NADH (2 mM) to a solution containing 10 μM of the probe. The fluorescence signal of probe 11 (10 μM) increased with increasing concentrations of NADH (0–800 μM), with a detection limit of 1.2 μM. Probe 11 had good sensitivity and excellent selectivity for NADH and NADPH. The fluorescence signal increased in the presence of NADH in the pH range of 4–10, indicating that probe 11 could be applied in physiological environments with different pHs. Probe 11 exhibited low toxicity against MDA-MB-231 cells, even at a concentration of 30 μM for 6 h, as evidenced by an MTT assay. Colocalization experiments with ER-Tracker, Lyso-Tracker, and Mito-Tracker showed that probe 11 could target mitochondria in living cells. When MDA-MB-231 cells were preincubated with glucose (20 mM) and carbonyl cyanide 4-(tri-fluoromethoxy)phenylhydrazone (FCCP) (5 μM), and then treated with probe 11, cells preincubated with glucose displayed the strongest fluorescence signal, followed by untreated cells, while cells preincubated with FCCP displayed the weakest fluorescence intensity. This was likely because glucose could promote the production of NADH through glycolysis, whereas FCCP disrupted ATP synthesis, resulting in reduced NADH levels through the activity of NADH dehydrogenase. Finally, probe 11 was used to distinguish cancer cells (MDA-MB-231) from normal cells (NIH-3 T3). NIH-3 T3 cells were prestained with calcein AM, which exhibits a very strong green fluorescence signal. Next, NIT-3 T3 cells prestained with calcein AM and MDA-MB-231 cells were mixed and incubated for 24 h, followed by incubation with probe 11 for 2 h. Compared with NIH-3 T3 cells, MDA-MB-231 cells showed a strong red fluorescence signal. Additionally, MDA-MB-231 cells and nonlabelled NIH-3 T3 cells stained with probe 11 in the individual dishes showed similar staining intensity, indicating that probe 11 may have the potential to discriminate cancer cells from normal cells based on differences in the fluorescence intensity of probe 11. This probe could be used to investigate both physiological and pathological processes in cells through the detection of NADH and NADPH in mitochondria.

Based on different recognition units of quinone, resazurin, benzophenoxazine, quinolinium and pyridinium, a diverse of fluorescent probes for the detection of NADH and NADPH have been developed. There still exist some limitations. The quinone-based probe needed the addition of the iridium (Ir) complex as a promoter, which restricts their biological applications due to the potential toxicity of the metal compound. The response of resazurin-based fluorescent probes toward NADH and NADPH should be carried out at an alkaline environment or in the presence of the boronic ester group to accelerate the sensing process, which is unfavorable for living biosystems. Benzophenoxazine-based fluorescent probe showed a fluorescent turn-off response. Quinolinium and pyridinium are among the most widely employed recognition units for the NADH and NADPH fluorescent probes, which have benefits including fluorescent turn-on response, and good biocompatibility. However, when the quinolinium moiety was connected to electron-donating groups (e.g., an amino group), the recognition reaction was relatively slow, which should be taken into consideration in the future design of quinolinium-based fluorescent probes.

Changes in NADH and NADPH content in cells and in vivo are closely related to numerous biological metabolic processes under both normal and pathological conditions. In this review, recent advances in the development of fluorescent probes for the detection of NADH and NADPH in living cells and in vivo were summarized according to different recognition units. These mentioned studies provided effective tools for use in the detection of NADH and NADPH, as well as novel ideas for research into both physiological and pathological processes. This review is expected to inspire the production of more fluorescent probes for the detection of NADH and NADPH. It could also stimulate research attention in the development of novel fluorescent probes based on carbon dots and nanoparticles, as well as metal complex-based, time-gated luminescent probes for monitoring NADPH and NADPH-related physiological and pathological processes, such as glycolysis, as well as for disease diagnosis and treatment. To attain high temporal and spatial resolution, more effective imaging techniques like light-sheet microscopy, NIR II microscopy, super-resolved fluorescence microscopy, two-photon microscopy, and photoacoustic microscopy, are also expected to be employed in the future.

Pengjuan Sun: Investigation, Writing - original draft. Hongxing Zhang: Investigation, Writing - original draft, Funding acquisition. Yuanqiang Sun: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Jing Liu: Conceptualization, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision.
